data.title: Dutch Bros Is Growing Fast, So Is The Market Cap
data.description:
Dutch Bros (NYSE:BROS) continues to impress with rapid expansion and strong revenue growth, but the stock looks like it’s already sprinted a bit ahead of the fundamentals. I’m encouraged by the company’s operational performance, particularly with the positive same-store sales and the fast-paced rollout of new locations. However, after nearly doubling over the past year, shares now trade near all-time highs and at valuation multiples far above even best-in-class peers like Starbucks (SBUX) and McDonald’s (MCD). If we add to that the continued reliance on share issuance to fund growth, the investment case starts to feel stretched. It’s a great business, no doubt, but at this price, it is only going to be worth it if you are a truly long-term investor.

Another Great Quarter

Let me start by highlighting the impressive top-line strength. Revenue in Q1 sat at $355.2 million, a 29.1% increase year-over-year. That growth was driven by continued shop openings and a bounce in same-store sales. In the last quarter, Dutch Bros opened 30 new shops, pushing the total to 1,012, after opening its 1000th shop on February 7 in Orlando, Florida.

Same shop sales showed a significant uplift, growing by 6.9%, up from the prior quarter, which tells me that demand is holding up despite some of the shakier macro backdrop we have seen recently. For me, this is quite some achievement given that a titan such as Starbucks reported U.S. comparable store sales declining 2%. According to management, this improvement was a result of better labor scheduling, stronger promotions, and with the help of a smoother digital ordering experience, which all boosted throughput and ticket size. The introduction of more streamlined beverage production practices and optimized menu displays also contributed to throughput gains, particularly during peak hours.

Profitability also showed signs of progress in my opinion. Adjusted EBITDA grew 19.7% to $62.9 million, and GAAP net income was $22.5 million. The company ended the quarter with adjusted EPS of $0.14, compared to $0.09 in Q1 of last year.

However, not all of it was roses in that 3 months. The input costs have remained elevated, and while management has made progress on inventory discipline and scheduling efficiency, the benefits have not yet translated into broader margin expansion.

Some of those cost headwinds are starting to bite into the operational efficiency gains. Shop-level margins for company-run stores held at 21.9%, about flat versus last year, largely as a result of the 100 basis points increase in labor cost for the quarter. Yet, I think the scale story largely remains intact, as we are about to see the best of the business.

Expansion Is on Track

So far, Dutch Bros is executing the playbook very well. Management reaffirmed the target of 160 new shops in 2025, and the pace suggests they’re on track. Unit economics also look solid, with AUVs above $2 million in the mature base and new shop paybacks around two years. In several western states, payback periods have even dipped below 20 months, signaling robust local demand and operational efficiency. That number could even drop further if the management succeeds in dropping the costs of opening stores from $1.7 million in 2024 to $1.25 million in the future.

They’re also clearly investing in the systems needed to support scale. There has been a lot of effort dedicated to better training, retention initiatives, and digital upgrades. The recently implemented workforce management software has started to yield better labor utilization metrics, and new broista onboarding programs are improving customer satisfaction scores. I still think the “broista” culture is a real asset, especially in their core Western markets, where the brand has deep roots and high recognition.

However, as Dutch Bros pushes eastward, I’m starting to question how well that culture can scale. Management downplayed the softer-than-expected ramp-up in some newer Southeastern markets, but I’m watching that closely. Not every region will mirror the early success of Arizona or Oregon, as you can see from the picture below.

I also wonder whether the brand's heavy reliance on the drive-thru format can achieve the same loyalty and throughput in colder or more urbanized climates where drive-thru usage may be lower.

Confident Guidance

Management’s guidance paints a picture of confidence, but I’m more cautious.

The long-term roadmap to 2,029 locations by 2029 looks well within reach, if the business increases the store openings each year by about 20%, which is not an easy task at all. Since about two-thirds of the shops are company-operated, Dutch Bros gets to control more of the customer experience, but that also means it bears more of the labor and operational burden, too.

If we look at the numbers provided in the last report, the guidance for the full year remains unchanged. Revenue is still expected between $1.15 billion and $1.17 billion, and adjusted EBITDA should land between $205 million and $215 million. Capex guidance remains elevated as well, with most of the spend tied to real estate and construction for new shops, commissaries, and regional distribution hubs. Free cash flow is still expected to be negative for 2025, with 2026 expected to be the first real turning point.

Overall, the CEO, Christine Barone, highlighted the company’s ability to adapt if inflation or regional softness emerges. The executive team believes that improvements in supply chain reliability and vendor consolidation efforts will help support margins even better in the second half of the year. But as the expansion continues at this pace, and profitability remains a few years out, the valuation starts to matter more. With shares already rallying sharply over the past year, the question becomes not whether Dutch Bros can grow into its goals, but whether that growth is already more than priced in.

A Premium That’s Hard to Justify

Shares of Dutch Bros have surged about 76% over the past 12 months and are up over 33% year-to-date, making it one of the top-performing names in the restaurant and beverage space. Outperforming titans such as Starbucks and McDonald's by as much as 60% in the last year.

The stock is currently leaning towards the high end of its historical range and hovers not far from the average analyst price target of $78, implying limited upside from here. Especially when we look at what multiples the business is trading at.

What makes this more difficult to reconcile is the sheer magnitude of the valuation premium. Dutch Bros trades at nearly 117x forward earnings, numbers that far exceed what even best-in-class consumer discretionary companies typically command. Starbucks, for example, trades at 37x forward earnings, McDonald’s at 23.6x, and even newer growth names like Luckin Coffee fetch far lower multiples, such as 21x net earnings, relative to their growth trajectories and margin profiles.

The same is true if we compare the business based on a forward EV/EBITDA basis, where Dutch Bros trades at over 35x. That’s way higher than Starbucks at 22x, and nearly double McDonald's 17.8x. But here we have to take into account how fast the business is growing, 6% same-store sales growth compared to negative 2% for Starbucks, as mentioned before.

It’s not that Dutch Bros doesn’t deserve a premium, I am pretty sure it does. After all, the business seems to be doing much better than its peers, despite not having their scale yet. The question is how much of a premium is too much.

That’s why I ran a projection based on Dutch Bros’ own growth targets. By 2029, the company plans to reach 2,029 stores across the United States. If we assume that the business manages to add another 15% increase in average unit sales over the next five years (factoring in inflation and operating improvements), revenue could potentially reach roughly $3.04 billion. That estimate is slightly below the analyst consensus range of $3.24 billion to $3.59 billion, with a midpoint of $3.41 billion.

Supporting that topline growth is a margin structure that continues to improve. According to the data, Dutch Bros has already surpassed Starbucks on gross margin, and its EBITDA margin currently stands at about 15.4%. By 2029, I believe Dutch Bros could reasonably achieve EBITDA margins north of 20%, especially with the planned introduction of food offerings beginning in fiscal 2026, however we would use the more conservative 20% EBITDA margins for our valuation. On $3.04 billion in revenue, that would translate to about $608 million in EBITDA.

Using Starbucks’ current EV/EBITDA multiple as a base and adding a bit of a premium, I modeled the potential market cap for Dutch Bros in 2029. Even after accounting for the recent run-up in the stock and assuming no further multiple expansion, the company still has room to potentially double its market capitalization over the next five years. That would represent a CAGR of 15%, which is a very decent return, but nothing extraordinary at this point, especially when viewed against the elevated expectations currently embedded in the stock.

That said, it's important to distinguish between company value and shareholder return. My estimates speak to where the business could be valued in five years, but that doesn’t guarantee similar upside for the stock. And that brings us to what really concerns me about the stock.

Risks: Scaling Costs, Share Dilution, Tariff Exposure, and Market Saturation

Dutch Bros faces a unique set of risks, which I will start pointing out one by one.

First is the cost structure. Scaling at this pace, 200 stores per year on average over the next five years, comes at a price. In the past three years, Dutch Bros has been issuing a significant number of shares. To be precise, in 36 months, shares in issue have grown about 2.5x. That kind of dilution is hard to ignore. Even if Dutch Bros were to slow the pace and double its share count “only” by 2029, that dilution alone could significantly diminish shareholder returns, even if operating results continue to improve.

Tariffs are the most pressing near-term risk. The company hasn’t raised prices yet, but if import costs increase, it may have to eat the hit or pass it on to customers, neither of which is ideal in a competitive market. Unlike Starbucks, which operates a far more global and diversified business with a more balanced revenue mix and lower capital intensity, Dutch Bros remains highly concentrated in the U.S. That exposes it more directly to U.S. wage inflation, food input volatility.

We also cannot ignore the question about long-term saturation. The company’s goal of 7,000 units isn’t implausible, considering Starbucks, which has already surpassed 16,000 stores in the U.S. Furthermore, while performance in the West has been strong, newer Southeastern markets are still ramping slowly. If the brand pushes into regions that lack the same drive-thru culture or customer familiarity, systemwide AUVs could decline over time. Those risks seem a bit more distant, but they will grow more relevant in the next decade.

Lastly, valuation itself is also a risk. There’s a clear path to underperformance if any piece of the story stumbles. With expectations so high, the market won’t be forgiving, and any miss, whether that would be on same shop sales, margins, or unit openings, it could result in a sharp re-rating and significant multiples contraction.

Great Company, At a High Price

Dutch Bros remains an impressive business with enormous potential. It’s one of the most capable operators in the U.S. beverage space, with strong brand equity and a compelling long-term growth strategy. But the continued pace of share dilution makes it difficult to forecast reliable per-share returns. I’d welcome more clarity from management, particularly a timeline for when equity issuance will slow or stop. That kind of signal would go a long way in restoring confidence around long-term value creation.

In my view, Dutch Bros is best suited for patient, long-term investors who are comfortable with short-term volatility and willing to wait for operating leverage.

data.title: Allete: Delay In Transaction Closing, 14% ROR Opportunity
data.description:
ALLETE: Investment Thesis

On Feb. 7, 2025, I published an article, "ALLETE, Inc.: 11% Return Beats A Bank For Short-Term Idle Cash". I included a copy of an Excel spreadsheet showing how a purchase of ALLETE stock could provide a fairly assured ~11% return through June 30, 2025, assuming receipt of all necessary approvals and close of the share sale transaction by that date.

The share sale transaction is now not expected to close until later in 2025 and due to the extended duration, any share purchases back in February 2025 will likely have a reduction in potential rate of return to slightly better than mid single digits. At the same time, additional share purchases around the current share price level can be expected to achieve double-digit rates of return, providing the transaction closes by the end of 2025.

Background

In my June 30, 2024, article, "ALLETE, Inc.: BlackRock's Backdoor Takeover", with the share price at $62.57, I discussed how shareholders in ALLETE could earn 12.3% per annum by holding through mid-2025. This was predicated on a successful outcome of the bid for all the shares of the company at $67 cash per share expected to settle in mid-2025, subject to shareholder and various regulatory approvals. The offer also indicated dividends would continue to be paid through to settlement.

Excerpted from the ALLETE, Inc. (NYSE:ALE) 2023 Annual Report,

On May 6, 2024, ALLETE and a partnership led by Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (“CPP Investments”) and Global Infrastructure Partners (“GIP”), (the “partnership”), jointly announced that they have entered into a definitive agreement under which the partnership will acquire ALLETE for $67.00 per share in cash, or $6.2 billion including the assumption of debt (the "Merger"). The transaction is expected to close in mid-2025 and is subject to the approval of ALLETE’s shareholders, the receipt of regulatory approvals including by the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission ("MPUC"), the Public Service Commission of Wisconsin ("PSCW") and the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission ("FERC"), and other customary closing conditions.

On Feb. 7, 2025, I published article, "ALLETE, Inc.: 11% Return Beats A Bank For Short-Term Idle Cash". I included a copy of an Excel spreadsheet showing how a purchase of ALLETE stock could provide a fairly assured ~11% return through June 30, 2025, assuming receipt of all necessary approvals and close of the share sale transaction by that date.

Status of Required Regulatory Approvals for Proposed Transaction

On December 19, 2024, the company filed an 8-K announcing,

ALLETE receives FERC approval for the proposed transaction with the Canada Pension Plan Investment Board and Global Infrastructure Partners. Expects transaction to close in mid-2025.

Since publication of above linked article, ALLETE announced on March 13, 2025, approval of the proposed transaction by the Public Service Commission of Wisconsin, and further advised, "... ALLETE continues to work on securing the last major regulatory approval for the transaction from the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission (MPUC)... ALLETE expects to complete the transaction in mid-2025, subject to remaining regulatory approvals and other customary closing conditions. "

First Indication of Delay to Close of Proposed Transaction

At the ALLETE 2024 Annual Meeting on 13 May 2025, ALLETE CEO, Bethany Owen, advised the share sale transaction remains on track for a 2025 closing, subject to all necessary approvals. The CEO further advised that the regulatory process with the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission ("MPUC") continues, and we expect a meeting before the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission later this fall.

Fall in the United States will begin on September 22nd, 2025, so the proposed transaction can not now close until sometime during Q4-2025.

Share Sale Transaction Termination Rights

Excerpted from 6 May 2024 8-K filing,

The Merger Agreement contains certain termination rights for both the Company and Parent, including if the Merger is not consummated by August 5, 2025 (subject to extension for an additional two successive three-month periods if all of the conditions to closing, other than the conditions related to obtaining regulatory approvals, have been satisfied). The Merger Agreement also provides for certain termination rights for each of the Company and Parent, and provides that, upon termination of the Merger Agreement under certain specified circumstances, Parent would be required to pay a termination fee of $212 million or $164 million (depending on the specific circumstances of termination) to the Company, and under other specified circumstances, the Company would be required to pay Parent a termination fee of $116 million.

The extension provisions allow for the closing to be deferred beyond August 5, 2025 out to February 5, 2026, so there does not appear to be any cause for concern at present the transaction might not proceed.

Termination by the Company (ALLETE)

As the shareholders have approved the sale, I expect this is no longer an option.

Termination by the Parent (buyer)

ALLETE has ~58 million shares on issue, so if the Parent (buyer) were to terminate in circumstances where a termination fee of $164 million or $212 million became payable to the company, the benefit to ALLETE shareholder funds would be around $2.82 to $3.65 per share.

Updated and New Rate of Return Projections

Rate of return through the date of close of the proposed transaction will be driven by share price at date of purchase, subsequent dividend receipts, the dollar uplift between share purchase price and the $67 share sale price, and the duration of the investment between purchase date and the date of cash receipt following the close date. The longer the duration of the investment, the greater the dilution of rate of return due to uplift of the share price.

Updated Rate of Return Projections

At the time of my previous article, the expected rate of return of ~11% assumed a share purchase price of $65.62 and a transaction close by June 30, 2025. Given the advice from the company the MPUC hearing is not expected until later this fall, I am now assuming transaction close will not occur ahead of say October 23, 2025, 30 days after commencement of this fall. This is interesting, as in the normal course of events, an increased quarterly dividend would be declared around October 25, 2025. I have extended the Table 1 spreadsheet calculations from my previous article out to 31 December 2025. If the $67 cash price was settled on October 23, ahead of the October 25 dividend declaration, a rate of return of 6.67% would be achieved from a purchase of ALLETE stock at market price of $65.62 on Feb. 7, 2025. Settlement a month later on November 25, when the stock had gone Ex-Div would increase the rate of return to 7.21%. Settlement on 31 December 2025 would result in a lower rate of return of 6.50% due to the longer duration of the investment.

New Rate of Return Projections - Current Share Purchase

From my previous article -

"Don't expect the share price to exactly track Table 1 daily balances - but that presents opportunities"

The indicated return of ~11% per previous article will not be achieved because the company has subsequently advised the anticipated mid-year close will not be met. However, the company is indicating close of the transaction is still expected in 2025. The market has obviously reacted to the deferral of the anticipated close date, with a fall in the share price to the current $63.53 at close on 20 June 2025. This provides an opportunity for a rate of return of 14.18% from a current purchase of ALLETE shares based on settlement of the proposed $67 per share occurring on 31 December 2025 (see Table 1 below). Of course, the risk of transaction close being further deferred beyond the end of 2025 remains. As discussed above, earlier settlement of the $67 per share would result in a higher rate of return than 14.18% due to the shorter duration of investment.

Table 1

Table 1 above shows how a purchase of ALLETE stock at the current price of $63.53 per share could be expected to provide ~14.18% return assuming the proposed $67 per share is received on 31 December 2025.

Think of Table 1 as a bank statement, with an interest rate of 14.18% per year compounding on a daily basis. A deposit of $63.53 today, with two withdrawals of $0.73 each as indicated, and the daily balances and ending balance of $67.00 as shown in Table 1 will be the result (note that interest calculations in Table 1 take account of dividend receipt occurring later than ex-div date).

Don't expect the share price to exactly track Table 1 daily balances - but that presents opportunities

Table 1 above shows the projected ALLETE share price assuming uniform growth at the 14.18% rate. But that is not how markets behave. If on any day in the future the share price falls below the price for that day indicated in Table 1, that could be an opportunity to buy for a better return than buying at the current price, provided of course, the expected settlement date and other assumptions do not change.

ALLETE: Summary and Conclusions

The share price at close on June 20, 2025, was $63.53, and if the sale proceeds as currently indicated, a payment of $67 per share will be made to shareholders by the end of 2025. The company has indicated dividends will continue to be paid in the interim, so dividends totaling at least $1.46 will also be received (there could be an increase in the quarterly dividend rate based on past actions). A shareholder could sell now for $63.53 and put the proceeds into a bank interest-bearing cash deposit. Alternatively, a shareholder could hold to receive two dividends of at least $0.73 each per share and wait for the payment of $67 per share, by 31 December 2025. As per Table 1 above, the equivalent daily compounding interest rate required from a bank cash deposit would need to be ~14.18% per year to match the potential return from the shareholder's ALLETE shares. That appears to be a fairly good return for what is likely a fairly low-risk option. A similar return could be earned by anyone buying ALLETE shares on the market at $63.53. Accordingly, I maintain a Hold recommendation for existing shareholders, and ALLETE would be a Strong Buy for potential new investors and existing investors looking for a fairly assured ~14.18% per year daily compounding return, for an investment maturing in ~6 months time. In the highly unlikely event the transaction does not close, stockholders would be left with shares in a solid utility company, with a dividend yield of 4.56%, and a forward P/E ratio of 17.00 (Sector median per SA Premium 17.59). Table 1 also provides prices payable on future dates which would achieve a similar ~14.18% return, although for a shorter duration of investment. Purchase of ALLETE stock at actual share prices below the share price for any particular day shown in Table 1 would result in returns greater than 14.18%, and vice versa. There is the risk the transaction might not close, either due to failure to achieve all regulatory approvals, or failure of the buyers to complete, but there appears to be no indication at present that either of these eventualities might occur. The greater risk could be a further delay in close beyond the end of 2025, and any increase in duration of investment would result in a reduction of the potential rate of return, as has already occurred since the date of the previous article linked above.

data.title: American Tower: Continued Upside Is Justified
data.description:
Back in February of this year, one company that I decided to revisit was telecommunications giant American Tower Corporation (NYSE:AMT). For a while, shares of the mammoth sized REIT had been under pressure. But through it all, I remained adamant that the company was an attractive play. This might seem peculiar to those who follow my work closely. As a value-oriented investor, I don't like the idea of paying a premium for businesses. However, the overall industry dynamics that the company should benefit from, as well as the steady growth, strong cash flows, and overall safety of the business, justified a bullish assessment. Since reaffirming it as a ‘buy’ candidate in that article, the stock has not disappointed. Shares have generated a return for investors of 9.3% at a time when the S&P 500 has been flat.

Looking at the most recent data available, I see a firm that continues to do well. Admittedly, some of its results have been a bit mixed. And this year might not be the best year ever for it. However, leverage for the company remains in check and is actually lower than other similar firms like Crown Castle (CCI) and SBA Communications (SBAC). And from a valuation standpoint, doesn't look bad. This is certainly not the kind of play that will make you rich. But over the long haul, I think that this makes for a good risk-adjusted investment for those who want a little bit of yield while simultaneously getting decent upside. Because of this, I have no problem keeping the company rated a soft ‘buy’ for now.

Taking a look at recent results

In my last article, I was able to cover data through the final quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. Results at the moment now extend through the first quarter of 2025. During the most recent quarter, revenue for the company was $2.56 billion. That was a bit above the $2.51 billion the company reported one year earlier. Property revenue for the company remained virtually flat year over year, inching up only slightly from $2.48 billion to $2.49 billion. The real driver was on the services side, with a surge from $30.2 million to $74.6 million. This growth was thanks to a rise in site application, zoning, and permitting activities, as well as certain construction management services, and structural and mount analyses services.

Even though property revenue for the company did not really move much higher, there was a part of the firm that actually did really well. This was the data center business. This represents a massive market opportunity in the era of AI. And, not wanting to miss out on it, management has made investments into it over the years. Year over year, data center revenue grew from $224.6 million to $244.1 million. This was thanks to greater rental, related, and other revenue stemming from new lease commencements, customer expansions, and higher rates than it charged customers when they renewed their contracts. A rise in power revenue and an increase in interconnection revenue, both of which were largely driven by higher pricing and increased demand from its users, ended up being responsible for this.

There have been some weak spots. For instance, property revenue in the US and Canada actually dipped from $1.31 billion to $1.30 billion. This is because of a reduction in straight line accounting that more than offset additional leasing activities, contractual rent escalations, and acquisitions that the company made. We did see growth in Europe, as well as in both Africa and the Asia Pacific region. But Latin America proved problematic. Revenue actually fell by 10.4% year over year from $445.5 million to $399.2 million. The good news is that this seems to be a temporary blip on the radar, with the decline attributable to foreign currency fluctuations. Namely, we are talking about the Brazilian Real and the Mexican Peso.

When it comes to bottom-line results, REITs are one area where net profits really aren't that important. But just for the sake of being comprehensive, year over year, net income did plummet from $830 million to $498.6 million. This actually stemmed from a massive drop in other income from $113.1 million to negative $338.2 million. This stemmed primarily from foreign currency fluctuations that negatively affected the company. By comparison, other parts of the firm are doing much better. Operating cash flow, for instance, rose from $1.28 billion to $1.30 billion. If we adjust for changes in working capital, it did admittedly fall from $1.55 billion to $1.50 billion. FFO, or funds from operations, also dropped, declining from $1.34 billion to $816 million. But on an adjusted basis, it actually rose from $1.20 billion to $1.29 billion. Lastly, EBITDA for the company grew from $1.71 billion to $1.74 billion.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year as a whole, management expects property revenue to be somewhere between $9.97 billion and $10.12 billion. Even at the low end, that would compare favorably to the $9.93 billion the company reported for last year. It's unclear what services revenue will look like since management has not provided guidance on that front. But they have provided a lot of guidance when it comes to profits and cash flows. Net income from continuing operations, for instance, should be between $2.775 billion and $2.875 billion. Management anticipates adjusted FFO of between $4.85 billion and $4.94 billion. On top of this, EBITDA is likely to come in at between $6.885 billion and $6.955 billion. In the chart above, you can see what these should look like at the midpoint of guidance and what FFO and adjusted operating cash flow should look like if they change the same rate that adjusted FFO is expected to at its midpoint.

Using these estimates, I then created the chart above. In it, you can see how the company is priced using four different trading multiples. And in the chart below, you can see how the firm stands up against two similar firms using two of the same valuation metrics. On a price to operating cash flow basis, it does appear to be the most expensive of the group. But it's in the middle of the group when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach. As I stated at the outset of this article, this is not a real value play necessarily. It's an expensive company. But what investors are paying for here is the stability of the firm and the lower risk than what other companies in other industries might experience.

Bolstering this argument is the net leverage ratio of the business. In the chart below, you can see the net leverage ratio for the company using historical results from 2024 and estimates for 2025. This assumes that the overall net debt picture of the business doesn't change from where it ended in the most recent quarter. On this basis, the firm is quite a bit below what the other two players that I compared it to are at. So that certainly deserves some degree of optimism.

Of course, the overall net debt picture of the company could change. In fact, it likely will to some extent. After all, in May of this year, management closed on the issuance of $562 million of senior unsecured notes that are now going to come due in 2032. Management mentioned that they would use these proceeds, which would be $558.4 million on a net basis, to repay their multi-currency credit facility. But at the end of the most recent quarter, that amounted to only $50 million. They mentioned using it for other corporate purposes. But that could honestly mean anything. It would be interesting to see the firm tackle some of its higher interest debt. For instance, it has some senior notes coming due in November 2028 that carry with them an annualized rate of 5.80%. The total amount there is $744.9 million. And with the new debt requiring only 3.625% per annum, there could be some interest savings there. But only time will tell what the company ultimately uses this capital for.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I still believe that American Tower Corporation is a fine firm. Since my goal is to outperform the broader market for the foreseeable future, it's not the kind of play that I would buy into. Or at least, I wouldn't at these levels. Having said that, this is a quality business that, in the long run, will likely do fine. Shares are not ridiculously priced. And that's especially true when you consider the quality that investors are buying into. Because of this, I believe that assigning the company a soft ‘buy’ rating is appropriate.

data.title: Rekor Systems: New Deals And New Management Structure To Help Jolt This Company Back To Life
data.description:
While the stock market continues to flirt with positive territory this year amid sharp macroeconomic uncertainty and tariff woes, a lot of the rebound has been concentrated in large-cap stocks, particularly in companies that boast tailwinds in AI. Meanwhile, investors still appear to have a risk-off attitude when it comes to small caps. And while it’s true that the sector isn’t uniformly appealing, careful stock selection can unearth some very attractive diamonds in the rough.

Rekor Systems (NASDAQ:REKR), in my view, is one of these names. The roadway intelligence software platform has been pummeled this year, with its stock down over 30% since the start of the year. But taking a fresh look at this stock now amid major new sales wins, we have to ask ourselves: how much of this selloff is already overdone?

I last wrote a buy opinion on Rekor in January, when the stock was trading near $1.80. In hindsight, my buy call was premature. At the time, I had praised the company’s innovative products, its large market opportunity built on mostly public sector clients, and its modest valuation. I didn’t predict the company’s Q1 revenue miss and for growth to contract so quickly, which tanked the stock. And yet with a new organization structure in place, alongside new major deals signed, I think there's more upside risk than downside risk in this stock at current levels, and I am renewing my buy call here.

There are two recent drivers that investors should be aware of for Rekor that can help push the stock higher. The first is that the company has decided to implement a new organizational structure based on general managers for each business unit. As a reminder for investors who are newer to this stock, Rekor consists of three main product lines: Rekor Scout (capturing vehicle license data), Rekor Command (for tracking vehicle speeds and reporting incidents), and Rekor Discover (an analytics platform that consolidates data from Rekor's other two divisions and produces data, such as on emissions statistics). In response to Rekor's declining profitability, the company has given the general managers of these segments P&L responsibilities over their respective units: increasing pressure for each unit to appropriately allocate resources and hit division-level profitability. We note that in Q1, despite a drop in revenue, Rekor was able to slim down its losses (more details on Q1 results in the next section).

The second recent catalyst is the fact that Rekor signed a major deal with the Texas Department of Transportation. The deal is up to eight figures in total size (quite significant for a company that generates less than $10 million in quarterly revenue), authorizing both urban and rural municipalities across the state of Texas to immediately adopt Rekor's tracking technology. We had been concerned about Rekor's revenue declines and, in particular, its decline in RPO balances that indicated the pipeline for new deals had run dry. Rekor's ability to sign a massive new deal with a major U.S. state helps to change the narrative and potentially act as the first domino in a string of new wins. We note that Rekor stock initially jumped as high at ~40% (to ~$1.60) on the early reaction to this news, but the stock has since given up all of those gains - despite the fact that this deal is still very much in play.

Stay long here: in my view, Rekor's rebound potential is drastically under-appreciated.

Q1 download

Of course, we do acknowledge that Rekor's latest quarterly results were disappointing, but there were bright spots to call out that help to affirm Rekor's transformation. The Q1 earnings results are shown below:

Rekor's revenue declined -6% y/y to $9.2 million, which dramatically missed against Wall Street's expectations of $10.9 million (+11% y/y). Now, to a large extent, the erosion in Rekor's revenue base was predictable from its remaining performance obligations, which I had called out at the beginning of the year for declining double digits. Essentially, the company's deferred revenue backlog was running low, indicating that the company needed to sign new deals to keep up its growth momentum.

The chart below disaggregates Rekor's revenue by type. We find comfort in the fact that Rekor's recurring revenue (earned from subscription fees to its SaaS platform) actually still grew at 2.9% y/y to $5.1 million, or 55% of Rekor's total. Where it flailed, however, was in "product and services revenue" - which is typically earned in the form of one-time implementation and setup fees, typically to new customers. This revenue line also includes a small number of one-time perpetual license (i.e., not recurring SaaS contracts) deals.

The decline in product and services revenue (but with growth in recurring revenue) is a clear indicator, as was the company's declining RPO balances, of weak new deal momentum. That's where we find plenty of hope in the Texas Department of Transportation deal announced in June, which has the potential to be very meaningful to Rekor's P&L. We caution that it's an eight-figure deal over a multi-year period - but even if we conservatively assume the bare minimum of $10 million over 5 years, a ~$2 million annual revenue bump from this one customer alone is a significant portion of Rekor's ~$40 million in annualized revenue. In other words, though Rekor's new business momentum had been disappointing through the first quarter, we already have evidence of the beginnings of a turnaround.

We note as well that despite revenue declines, Rekor reduced its adjusted EBITDA losses by -21% y/y to -$7.4 million.

The company notes that its new GM structure is designed to encourage faster decision-making and emphasize segment profitability for each of Rekor's units. Per CEO Robert Berman's remarks on the Q1 earnings call regarding the new structure:

The shift to a GM structure led organization is designed to deliver several strategic benefits: greater accountability, where each GM will be responsible to build a team that focuses on sustainable growth within their own segment, enhanced innovation. By giving them more control, each unit will be able to concentrate on innovations that are most valuable to their customers on a near-term basis. Top priority in product development will be given to customer value, improved customer focus, by concentrating more intensively on the needs of new and existing customers, our teams will be more focused on learning what their current needs are rather than imagining what their needs might be in the future; scalable revenue growth. By concentrating on distinct product lines and regions, each unit can pursue growth opportunities more effectively."

We hope that, on top of a large multi-product deal and a new emphasis on profitability, Rekor can quickly begin to improve its loss profile.

Risks, valuation, and key takeaways

Of course, there are a number of risks that investors should be aware of when it comes to investing in a small-cap, unproven stock like Rekor. The size of Rekor's deal with Texas, though an encouraging signal for future growth, serves to highlight that Rekor's deal wins tend to be larger, lumpy, and complex. In a tougher macro environment, these massive deals may be hard to come by - in other words, Rekor's momentum has been choppy in the past, and we should expect it to continue being choppy in the near future.

I also think the general manager structure, while having advantages in incentivizing unit profitability, may also reduce cohesion and cross-selling between units. If each GM is concerned only with his or her own unit's sales and bottom line, the company may lose the coordination that may help land large, multi-product deals like the Texas win.

That being said, I think these risks are already more than well reflected into Rekor's current valuation. At current share prices just above $1, Rekor trades at a market cap of $137.0 million. After we net off the $3.9 million of cash and $15.9 million of debt on the company's latest balance sheet, Rekor's resulting enterprise value is $149.0 million.

For the current year FY25, Wall Street analysts have a consensus revenue target of $49.0 million for the company, representing 7% y/y growth. This puts Rekor's valuation at just 3.0x EV/FY25 revenue.

I wouldn't bet the proverbial farm on this small-cap stock, as it certainly contains a number of risks we can't ignore: but at the same time, a new org structure that emphasizes profitability and a major new client that can push the company back to revenue growth are great catalysts for this stock to stage a near-term rebound rally.

data.title: Ramaco Resources: Lean Miner, Heavy Lifting, But Still Waiting On A Break
data.description:
You don’t hear much about Ramaco (METC), and maybe that’s a feature, not a bug. Quiet operator. Mostly met coal. Keeps its head down in Appalachia. Doesn’t chase headlines. Just mines, cuts checks, and minds the balance sheet. But recently, they’ve gone off-script, now trying to wring rare earths out of old coal ground in Wyoming. Maybe brilliant, maybe speculation. Either way, the market hasn’t priced it in, probably for a reason.

Q1 2025 Results

Ramaco pulled in $134.7 million in revenue this quarter, down from $172.7 million in Q1 2024. They sold more coal, 946k tons against 929k a year ago, but prices collapsed. Average realized price dropped from $155 to $122 per ton. If we add logistics it was $142/ton vs $186 last year, currently, met coal prices are down around 27% YoY give or take, and it shows.

Costs fell too. Cost of sales was $114.1 million (–18%), and ton costs dropped from $150 to $121. On a cash cost basis, they averaged $98/ton, down $20 YoY. They still make money on each ton, just not enough to get green in sheets.

Operating loss came in at –$12.0 million. Last year same quarter they posted +$3.3 million. Interest expense jumped to $2.2 million (from $1.3 million) mainly due to issued notes on December 2023. Bottom line: net loss of $9.46 million or –$0.19/share. Adjusted EBITDA was $9.8 million, down from $24.2 million.

Cash flow from ops was decent, $26 million compared to $25.2 million last year. CapEx was heavy as expected, $20.3 million compared to $18.7 million. After investments they came out roughly flat. Ops cash basically covered the spend. Ended the quarter with $44.3 million in cash, $88.4 million in notes, $16 million on the revolver, and $20 million in lease. Net debt’s still low and the balance sheet’s clean. Current ratio ~1.3, debt/equity ~0.23. So yeah, liquidity’s not a worry here and it's good.

Ramaco’s coal segment is still the engine of the business. They mined just over 1 million tons in Q1, it was a record, even with some weather issues in January and February that took about 100k tons off the table. They sold 946k tons in the quarter, a slight increase from last year, and about two-thirds of that went to export markets. The rest stayed domestic, mostly to North American steelmakers.

Domestic fixed-price contracts held up better, around $152/ton, while export tons slipped to ~$111/ton. With two-thirds of volume heading overseas, that did hurt. Overall realized price was $122/ton. Still, the company had about 3.7 million tons in sales locked in for 2025 by the end of Q1, with 2.2 million of those already priced at an average of $141. That gives them a decent floor for the rest of the year, not great, but better than spot exposure.

The cost side is what saved them from worse results. Ramaco runs tight. Cash costs dropped 17% YoY to $98/ton, which puts them in the first quartile among coal miners. The expansion of the Maben prep plant helped, and they’ve stayed disciplined on spending. Even though prices fell, they squeezed out a $24/ton margin – best in the peer group, but that's according to management.

SG&A stayed basically flat at $14.6 million, up slightly from $14.1 million last year. Depreciation ticked up to $17.5 million. CapEx came in heavier than last year ($20.3 million compared to $18.7 million), but most of that went to prep plant upgrades and mine development. Nothing wasted. Despite higher spending, operating cash flow was more than enough to cover it.

Risks and Catalysts

First thing that could move the stock is coal pricing. Doesn’t take much even a $10–15/ton bump would land straight in margin. Ramaco’s got the output and tight ops to soak that up fast. They’ve already locked in some 2025 tons at solid levels, so they’ve got a floor if markets stay shaky.

Then there’s Brook. They’ve got the $6.1 million Wyoming grant, the pilot plant is coming, and if it works, that changes everything. It turns them from a coal name into something more strategic. But we’re not there yet. Still early. Still a science project. But if it does work, it’s a serious kicker — and yeah, I don’t think that’s priced in at all.

Coal-wise, they’ve still got room to grow. Maben and Elk Creek can add volume without blowing out costs. And if U.S. steel picks up, or China throws out some stimulus, you get a tailwind.

There’s risk all over this. If coal stays soft or drops further, it hurts. They run lean, but they still bleed if the price isn't there. Most of their stuff goes export, and seaborne coal hasn’t been kind lately. If China stays slow, pricing’s stuck.

Brook’s also risky. It’s early, pre-revenue, unproven. If the tech doesn’t hold up or the economics are off, it stalls. It’s not a traditional project either — coal ore REEs aren’t common, and the processing path is tricky. High spends, unclear payoff. Could be something, could be nothing. That’s the gamble.

Valuation

Right now, Ramaco trades like the market doesn’t believe in either side of the story, not the coal cash flow, and not the rare earth optionality. P/E based on trailing numbers is around 17–18×. EV/EBITDA, depending on how you annualize it, is around 14–18×. That’s not cheap, especially for a cyclical business posting quarterly losses.

Free cash flow yield isn’t huge either. Q1 cash flow minus CapEx gives about $6 million in FCF. Multiply that by four and you get maybe $24 million /year. On a ~$620 million market cap, that’s ~3.9% yield. Decent, but not enough to move.

Dividend? They do pay one, but it’s complicated. Class A gets paid in shares. Class B, which is mostly insiders, gets cash. The cash dividend is around 2–3% annualized based on recent payout. It’s fine, but not something that should attract value buyers.

Bottom line, the stock isn’t priced like it has any rare earth upside. Brook is being treated as a science project until proven otherwise. On the coal side, valuation looks fair if prices stay where they are. But if prices recover, this could re-rate fast. If Brook starts delivering, same thing.

Summary

Ramaco’s holding steady. They’re running lean, not lighting cash on fire, keeping tons moving. On the coal side, they’re doing what they can in a bad market. Got some forward deals locked, balance sheet looks fine, operations are tight. But even so, they’re still bleeding. Brook? Could be the big one — or not. Right now, it’s mostly talk, pilot plants, maybe some rocks in a lab. If coal gets a pop or Brook actually proves something, sure, it could rerate. But today? It’s just kind of stuck. Not cheap enough to pull in deep value guys, not clear enough to hook growth folks. HOLD feels right. Just keep it on the radar.

data.title: Willdan Group: No Margin Of Safety For A Turnaround In Progress
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Willdan Group Inc. (WLDN) is a turnaround story. Its strategic shift to a national, integrated service model has begun to pay off, with post-2022 revenue driven by organic growth and improved margins.

The company benefits from strong macro tailwinds; however, historical underperformance in profitability, peer-relative metrics, and capital efficiency tempers the outlook. While recent trends are encouraging, the recovery is still in its early stages. At current valuation, there is no margin of safety.

Strategic Pivot

Over the past decade, Willdan transitioned from a public-sector-focused engineering firm to a nationwide provider of integrated energy, engineering, and software solutions.

  • In 2015, Willdan was a regional professional services firm primarily serving small to mid-sized public agencies. At that juncture, its business was structured around four segments: Engineering, Energy Efficiency, Public Finance, and Homeland Security.
  • In 2024, Willdan’s business focused on decarbonization, energy efficiency, and grid modernization. The company had consolidated its operations into 2 segments - Energy and Engineering & Consultancy Services.

As can be seen from Chart 1, the Energy segment was the biggest revenue contributor. In 2024, this segment accounted for about 84% of the total revenue. Willdan serves exclusively the US market.

Willdan's core business focuses on the following: energy services and utility programs. These are growth sectors as exemplified by the following:

“The global industrial energy-efficiency services market size is calculated at USD 13.58 billion in 2025…accelerating at a CAGR of 4.10% from 2025 to 2034… North America held the largest share…in 2024.” Precedence Research

“US grid modernization market size was valued at USD 12,445.2 million in 2024…at a CAGR of 17.93% during the forecast period (2024 to 2032).” Credence Research

“The Demand Side Management Market Industry is expected to grow… at a CAGR… 7.42% during the forecast period (2025–2034).” Market Research Future. Note: Demand-side management refers to strategies and programs implemented by utilities or governments to reduce or shift energy consumption by end-users.

Revenue Growth

Given the positive market outlook, you should not be surprised to find that Willdan’s revenue grew at 17.2% CAGR from 2015 to 2024. Looking at Chart 1, you can discern 2 periods with revenue growth spurts:

  • 2018 to 2020. Willdan experienced strong revenue growth driven primarily by a series of strategic acquisitions that expanded its geographic footprint and service offerings.
  • 2022 to 2024. This followed the post-pandemic recovery and coincided with a surge in federal infrastructure and clean energy funding, spurred by legislation like the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act and the Inflation Reduction Act.

The 2022 to 2024 revenue growth augurs well for Willdan, as it was driven largely by organic expansion rather than acquisitions. This momentum, combined with the projected market demand, suggests that Willdan could be positioned to sustain double-digit organic revenue growth in the immediate to short term.

Profitability

The company was able to translate the 17.2% CAGR in revenue into a slightly higher PAT growth of 20.4% CAGR. But as shown in the left part of Chart 2, profits were volatile.

Profits initially grew from 2015 to 2017 but declined after peaking in 2017 and only turned around in 2023. A big part of the profit growth came post-2022. Willdan’s PAT picture over the decade stemmed from a mix of:

  • Strong early-stage growth and operating leverage (2015–2017).
  • Acquisition-driven strain and pandemic disruptions (2018–2022).
  • Recovery on the back of organic growth and cost realignment (post-2022).

The return picture followed the profit picture as shown in the right part of Chart 2. Over the past decade:

  • ROIC ranged from negative 2.4% to positive 25.1% with an average of 7.5%.
  • ROE ranged from negative 8.6% to positive 17.2% with an average of 5.2%.

These average returns were lower than the current cost of equity and WACC of about 8.6% each, implying that the company failed to create shareholders’ value over the past decade.

The silver lining here is that returns have been on an uptrend post-2022. If these return trends can continue, the company will be able to start creating shareholders shortly.

Volatile Profits

I would posit that the post-2022 profit growth was driven by both higher revenue and fixed cost control.

  • As can be seen from Chart 3, there was not much margin expansion over the past decade. There were no improvement in the gross profit margin and contribution margin in 2024 compared to those in 2015.
  • While the Selling, General and Administration (SGA) margin in 2024 at 28% was lower than the 32% in 2015, it was volatile. As shown in the left part of Chart 3, the SGA margin only came under control post-2021.

Willdan’s stronger profit growth can also be attributed to its cost structure, where fixed costs make up roughly one-third of total costs. This operating leverage means that as revenue increases, a greater portion of the incremental income flows through to the bottom line, creating a multiplier effect on profits.

Notes to Chart 3: I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA and Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

Peer Comparison

I compared Willdan's performance with several engineering and consultancy companies that I have covered recently for Seeking Alpha. Refer to Table 1.

Willdan is among the small players in terms of 2024 revenue. But it had the best revenue growth rate from 2016 to 2024.

Notes to Table 1: The revenue growth was from 2016 to 2024 as Parsons’ data was available from 2016.

  • Jacobs Solutions Inc. (J). Jacobs is a global engineering, consulting, and project management firm delivering infrastructure, environmental, and energy solutions to government and commercial clients, with a growing focus on sustainability and climate resilience.
  • Limbach Holdings, Inc. (LMB). Limbach provides mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) construction and maintenance services, with specialized capabilities in energy-efficient building systems for institutional and commercial facilities.
  • Parsons Corporation (PSN). Parsons is a technology-driven solutions provider specializing in defense, intelligence, and critical infrastructure, with capabilities in smart mobility, cyber, energy transition, and federal contracting.
  • Tetra Tech, Inc. (TTEK). Tetra Tech is a leading provider of consulting and engineering services focused on water, environment, infrastructure, resource management, and sustainable energy solutions worldwide.

I compared Willdan’s 2016 to 2024 performances with those of its peers based on the following metrics. Refer to Charts 4 and 5.

Willdan's peer-relative performance declined significantly since 2016. It transitioned from a market leader to a laggard in key profitability and return metrics. While there is a positive signal from its free cash flow margin, this is not enough to offset consistent underperformance in return on capital, EBIT margin, and EPS growth.

  • Willdan started with the best return on capital. But this started to deteriorate relative to the peers, so that by 2020 it ranked the worst.
  • Willdan ranked No. 1 in terms of EBIT margin in 2016. Again, the performance declined so that by 2020, it had the worst EBIT margin.
  • Willdan’s free cash flow margin was volatile. There were 2 years that it was the top performer, and there were an additional 3 years when it was the worst performer. However, by 2024, it ranked No. 2 for this metric.
  • Willdan has the worst EPS growth rate from 2016 to 2024. And over the past 5 years, its annual EPS was ranked at the bottom or next to the bottom in terms of the quantum of EPS.

The findings suggest that while Willdan’s peer-relative performance was weak during 2018–2022, the business has undergone a strategic transformation, with:

  • A focus on fast-growing markets.
  • A more scalable, integrated business model.
  • Organic growth momentum.
  • Recovering profit margins and returns.
  • And improved free cash flow.

As such, there is a case that Willdan is positioned for a rebound in peer-relative rankings, particularly in EBIT margin, return on capital, and EPS growth, provided it sustains current execution trends.

Financial Position

I would rate Willdan as moderately sound financially, as its positive points more than balanced its negative ones. Its key positive points were:

  • Over the past decade, it generated positive cash flow from operations every year despite having 3 years of losses.
  • The company also had a good cash flow conversion rate. Over the past decade, it generated a total of USD 238 million in cash flow from operations compared to its cumulative USD 42 million profit after tax.
  • It has a 43% Debt-to-Equity ratio as of the end of March 2025. And this has come down from its 2019 high of 93%.
  • As of the end of March 2025, it had USD 38 million in cash, equal to about 8% of its total assets.

But the above positive points were partly offset by the following negative ones.

It did not have a good capital allocation plan, as shown in Table 2. You can see that the cash flow from operations was not enough to fund its CAPEX and acquisitions.

In its Q1 2025 presentation, the company stated that it expanded its credit facility by USD 200 million. I think this would provide the company with breathing space to manage its CAPEX and acquisitions.

It also had an unsustainable reinvestment rate (Reinvestment/NOPAT) that averaged 352% over the past decade. Over the long run, this has to reduce to one given by the fundamental growth equation of:

Growth = Return X reinvestment rate.

I defined reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisitions – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

To be fair, a bit of this high reinvestment rate was due to the low average NOPAT of USD 5 million per year compared to the average USD 19 million Reinvestment.

Valuation

Based on my analysis, I would rate Willdan as moderately sound from a fundamental perspective.

  • While the company showed signs of recovery in profitability and returns, it had a track record of underperformance in returns and peer comparisons.
  • Its ROIC, ROE, and margins have only just started to improve.

As such, in my valuation, I have adopted the following picture:

  • The revenue would be the 2024 revenue, growing at 15% in Year 1. This growth rate would reduce proportionately to 4% in the terminal year (Year 6). The 15% was based on the 2022 to 2024 CAGR in revenue of 14.8%. Note that the Q1 2025 net revenue grew by 24% year-on-year compared to the Q1 2024 revenue.
  • The base contribution margin would be the 2022/24 average margin and would improve proportionately to be 7% higher in the terminal year. Note that the contribution margin in 2024 was 7% higher than that in 2022.
  • The base fixed cost margin would be the 2022/24 average margin. I assumed that this would improve proportionately to be 14% lower in the terminal year. Note that the 2024 fixed cost margin was 14% lower than that in 2022.
  • The base capital efficiency would be the 2022/2024 average values.
  • The base reinvestment margin would be based on the past decade's average. This would be improved so that in the terminal year, the reinvestment rate would be governed by the fundamental growth equation.
  • The tax rate would be based on the past decade's average positive tax rate of 24.8%.

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 45 per share compared to its market price (18 Jun 2025) of USD 59 per share. There is no margin of safety.

Valuation model

I valued Willdan based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm [FCFF] model, as shown in Table 3.

The basic equations used in the model are:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) – Reinvestment.

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost

The contribution margin and fixed cost margins are based on the operating model shown in the right part of Chart 3.

The reinvestment was based on the reinvestment margin.

Notes to Table 3:

a. Assumed proportionate change

b. Pegged to the growth rate

c. Assumed proportionate change

d. Assumed proportionate change

e. Revenue X Margin, and after accounting for Fixed costs

f. Assumed proportionate change

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at the terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

o. Based on the number of shares

The WACC used in the valuation model was based on the first page results of a Google search for “Willdan Group WACC”. Refer to Table 4.

Valuation Risks

I think that my assumptions are optimistic, looking at the historical trends vs those projected by the valuation model.

  • The projected free cash flow has a steady growth trend compared to the historically volatile free cash flow margin. The projected trend is also much higher than that projected by the log normal trend line (shown in dotted line in the right part of Chart 6).
  • While I have projected a steeper revenue trend line compared to the past 3 years trend, the projected contribution margin appears reasonable. Refer to the left part of Chart 6.

I also found the following when comparing the terminal values projected by the valuation model to the historical values:

  • 16% return on capital compared to the 2023/24 average ROIC of 8%. The 16% would result in Willdan ranking No. 3 based on the 2024 peer returns.
  • 10% EBIT margin compared to the 2023/24 average EBIT margin of 5%. The 10% EBIT margin would result in Willdan ranking No. 2 based on the 2024 peer EBIT margins.

My point is that even with optimistic assumptions, there is no margin of safety.

The key challenge here is whether Willdan would be able to reduce its reinvestment rate to one governed by the fundamental growth equation. This would require the company to deliver significant improvements in capital efficiency. The track record has not been promising as there was no improvements in capital efficiency:

  • Over the past decade, asset turnover declined by 122% in 2024 compared to 187% in 2015.
  • Gross profitability (gross profits/total assets) also declined over the past decade. It was 44% in 2024 compared to 73% in 2015.

You may argue that, over the past 3 years (2022 to 2024), there were improving trends for these 2 metrics. But these improvements were not on the scale required. In my valuation model, the reinvestment rate declined from 257% in Year 1 to 25% in Year 6. Without this scale of declining reinvestment rate, the intrinsic value would be much lower.

Furthermore, in its Q1 2025 presentation, the company stated that it closed 2 acquisitions. This will make it more challenging to reduce the Reinvestments in the short term.

Conclusion

Willdan has demonstrated a clear strategic pivot over the past decade. It evolved from a regional public-sector consultancy into a national player focused on energy efficiency and infrastructure modernization.

This transformation has aligned the company with fast-growing sectors and led to a recent rebound in revenue, profitability, and returns. But all is not rosy.

The company's historical record reveals a pattern of underperformance in key metrics such as ROIC, EBIT margin, and EPS growth when compared to peers. As such, while financial results post-2022 have improved, there are still challenges for a sustained turnaround.

Willdan's capital efficiency remains a concern, as its historically high reinvestment rate and declining asset productivity cast doubt on its ability to grow without continued heavy capital demands.

My valuation reflects optimism about Willdan’s operational leverage, market positioning, and improving margin trends. Yet even under these favorable assumptions, there is no margin of safety.

data.title: Hafnia: Profitability And Value In Each Kilometer Sailed
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In a sector as volatile as shipping, where tariffs rise and fall at the rate of conflicts, inventories and the global macro, finding a company that combines profitability, financial stability and low relative risk is no easy task. Hafnia Limited (NYSE:HAFN) does not only succeed: it is proving it quarter by quarter, in the good years and in the quietest.

HAFN’s business is neither speculative nor ephemeral. The company transports refined products -petrol, diesel and jet fuel- between production centers and consumption centers around the world. It does so with a modern and well diversified fleet, under an operating model that privileges efficiency over volume, and which positions it as a reliable partner for the major global energy companies. It is a company that complies, even when the market becomes challenging.

Q1 2025 was just that: a less vibrant quarter, with more than 500 days of fleet down for scheduled maintenance. And yet, HAFN maintained its operating profitability, paid dividends, and closed the quarter with an enviable financial structure. Earned $63.2MM, distributed 80% of those profits, and maintained its LTV at just 24.1%. Even with more moderate rates and fewer active vessels, it continued to generate cash, adjusted its operations and made clear that its model works beyond cycle peaks.

The most striking, however, is not in its fleet, nor in its routes, nor even in its results. It is in its valuation. Today HAFN is traded with a P/E FWD of less than 9x, an EV/EBITDA below the industry average, and a dividend yield above 16%. In other words: it is a company that generates, distributes and grows, but the market still treats it as if it were at the bottom of the cycle. That mismatch is where the opportunity lurks.

In addition, HAFN is positioned to benefit from the structural changes going through the industry: less supply of new ships, longer routes due to geopolitical conflicts, regulatory pressure that favors efficient fleets and companies with higher standards.

For all this, I consider HAFN as a buy option. I see a company with a proven model, solid cash generation and valuation that does not reflect its true value or ability to grow. It is a clear opportunity for investors looking for recurring income via dividends and exposure to a strategic sector with favorable medium- and long-term perspectives.

Overview

On the vast map of global trade, few companies have a role as tangible-and at the same time invisible-as Hafnia Limited (HAFN). Based in Singapore and operating across all oceans, Hafnia handles something as essential as it is everyday: transporting the fuels that run the world. From gasoline and diesel, to jet fuel and chemicals, every load on one of its more than 200 tankers is a link in the global energy chain.

Hafnia is not just a fleet: it is an integrated platform. Manages not only own boats, but also third party boats, under one of the largest pool systems in the industry. This means that it combines operational efficiency with commercial flexibility, offering its customers-large energy companies, refiners and traders- agile and reliable logistics solutions. In addition, it is actively involved in bunkering (the supply of fuel for ships) and maritime brokerage, which positions it beyond simple transport.

Since its merger with BW Tankers in 2019, HAFN has only escalated. Today it competes with industry giants such as Scorpio Tankers (STNG) and Torm, with a global presence that allows it to connect the most relevant refining centers in the world -in the Middle East and Asia- with the main consumer markets in Europe, America and the rest of Asia. Its network is not only extensive, but strategic.

The sector’s outlook for 2025 is favourable. With fewer new boats entering the market (due to high costs and regulatory uncertainty), and a large part of the global fleet aging, companies like HAFN-with modern units, scalability and efficient models-start with advantage. In addition, realignment of logistics chains have revitalized the structural demand for transportation of refined products. Everything indicates that Hafnia is where it needs to be.

HAFN and the challenges in the Middle East and the Suez Canal

But 2025 did not come without a jolt. With the escalation of conflict in the Middle East, HAFN found itself at the center of a key geography for its operations.

The growing insecurity in the Strait of Hormuz and the Red Sea, due to the increasing tension between Iran, Israel and the Houthi rebels, increased the risk of transit on these routes and sharply increased freight costs: proof of this, within a few weeks, fees for LR2 vessels exceeded $51,000 per day, doubling previous values.

Many companies began diverting their routes along the Cape of Good Hope, a longer and more expensive route. The Suez Canal, a regular artery of maritime trade, was partially emptied. In an attempt to recover traffic, the canal authority offered 15% discounts to large container ships for a trial period of 90 days.

In parallel, sanctions on ships from Russia, Iran or Venezuela further complicated the normal flow of trade to strategic markets such as India and China. However, this same regulatory pressure could favor companies like HAFN, which operate under higher standards and focus on the "clean" segment of maritime transport

The threat of a closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which a quarter of the world’s oil flows, is now a real possibility. In this way, an escalation of the conflict could have a "huge" impact on global trade.

In this volatile context, HAFN emerged as a safe option. Its actions rebounded, driven by the search for reliable and regulated logistics solutions. Although the environment is challenging, the company was able to adapt, adjusting routes, optimizing operations and endorsing its role as an vital actor in an industry.

Q1 2025

The Q1 2025 found HAFN in a logical pause within a path that remains firm. With several vessels entering into planned maintenance, more than 500 days of fleet were temporarily out of service.

The impact was felt, as expected: net profit fell to $63.2MM, down 71% YoY from $219.6M in Q1 2024. But the important thing is that the business continued to show muscle where it really matters: profitability did not disappear, the box remained strong and shareholders continued to collect.

With fewer ships operating and slightly more moderate rates, adjusted EBITDA was $125.1 MM, 56% below last year. TCE revenue also decreased from $378.8 million to $218.8 million, with an average daily rate that dropped from $36,013 to $22,992.

Even so, HAFN did what few companies in cyclical sectors do: it maintained its commitment to shareholders. Paid a dividend of $0.1015 per share, representing 80% of its quarterly earnings, for a total of $50.6 MM. Yes, it was a lower payout than the previous year, but in context, it feels like a sign of stability and maturity, not weakness.

And speaking of stability, the balance was again present. With $188 MM in cash, a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio close to 1x and an LTV of 24.1%, HAFN continues to manage its capital with surgical precision. This kind of financial health not only allows you to endure a slack quarter, but gives you the freedom to choose when to grow, when to distribute and when to simply wait.

In addition, in Q1 2025, HAFN recorded a total of 9,514 operational days, with an average daily TCE of $22,992 and an average operating cost (OPEX) of $7,987 per day. The most profitable segment was the LR2, which with only 540 active days achieved a TCE of$33,911, the highest of all the fleet, standing out on long routes and higher value.

The LR1 brought volume and predictability, with 2,322 days and a TCE of $23,418, while the MR, core of the operation with 4,734 days, averaged $22,821, showing solid and stable margins. For their part, the Handys closed the quarter with the lowest TCE ($19,831), but also with the lowest daily OPEX ($7,611), which makes them efficient on shorter and regional routes.

The combination of these segments allowed to maintain a balanced operating performance, with a good differential between revenues and costs, even in a quarter conditioned by days off service and more moderate rates.

Valuation

Today HAFN is traded at a P/E FWD of 8.42x, well below the energy sector median (14.06x), and at a P/E TTM of just 4.71x, implying a discount of 65%.

Even in operational terms, its EV/EBITDA TTM is 4.97x, below the sector average (6.76x). Despite this low valuation, HAFN distributes a dividend with a return of 16.22%, largely covered by the FCF. The P/CF TTM ratio is only 3.07x, implying a highly efficient cash generation.

In terms of profitability, HAFN continues to outperform its peers. Net margin stands at 23.5%, compared with 8.4% for the sector. The ROE is 26.7%, almost three times the energy average, and the ROA is 16.7%, indicating very efficient use of productive assets.

Moreover, its CAPEX represents only 2.34% of sales, reflecting a light capital structure, and the leveraged FCF margin is 23.6%, among the highest in the entire industry.

In terms of growth, 2025 reflects a one-time decrease -due to tariff normalization and fleet maintenance- but long-term averages show real expansion. The company accumulated a CAGR of revenues of 43% in 3 years and 24.4% in 5 years, while EBITDA grew 74.3% in 3 years, and EBIT multiplied by 2.5 times in the same period.

The diluted EPS shows a compound growth of 28.6% over 5 years, and the net result adjusted for continuing operations grew by almost 39% in the same period. A transient fall of 51% in EPS is projected for 2025, but with an estimated rebound of 22% in 2026 and 14% in 2027.

The combination of multiple tablets, superior structural profitability and solid compound growth makes HAFN a clear value opportunity, which the market still does not fully recognize due to short-term cyclical noise.

Risks

HAFN is a company that sails with firm rudder and well-reinforced hull. But even the best designed boats face storms. And in this business, the waves don’t always tell.

The greatest threat, as always in maritime transport, is the cycle itself. Rates go up and down with a logic that sometimes responds to fundamentals, and other times to pure speculation or unexpected events. HAFN has proven to surf that volatility, but if demand for refined products cools down too long, margins adjust and the ability to continue delivering value is tested.

Nor can we ignore what happens across the ocean. Conflicts on critical routes such as the Red Sea or the Strait of Hormuz have led to diversions which, for now, favour the demand for tonnage. But the geopolitical risk is not gratuitous: delays, insurance costs and exposure to incidents rise, and that can change the rules of the game from one day to the next.

On the other hand, the energy transition is a race in which no one can stand still. New regulations on cleaner emissions and fuels are coming in strong. Hafnia is already taking concrete steps in this direction, but the reality is that adapting costs, and it is not always clear how much or at what pace.

Finally, although it now enjoys a solid balance sheet and fluid access to finance, the external conditions are not eternal. If interest rates remain high or the appetite for the sector cools, access to fresh capital may become less easy.

In short, Hafnia is not a fragile company. On the contrary: it is better prepared than many. But in the sea, no matter how much one knows the course, the weather always has the last word.

Conclusions

HAFN deserves a closer look. For investors who value companies that distribute with discipline, navigate with control and build real return without making noise, I consider HAFN to be a clear opportunity.

That’s why I think it is a good buy option, hoping for a normalization of multiples and market recognition to a model that combines efficiency, financial strength and exposure to favorable global dynamics. In a sector with changing tides, HAFN is that ship that keeps moving when others are just floating.

data.title: Exelon: Strong Buy Amid Data Center Expansion And Regulatory Tailwinds
data.description:
Data Center Supercycle

We initiate Strong Buy $68 PT - Exelon Corporation (NASDAQ:EXC) is a premier North American utility holding company with a diversified portfolio of Generation, Transmission and Distribution assets operating throughout a handful of large metropolitan markets. Beneath the shell of a traditional utility is a fundamentally inflecting business - Exelon sits at the intersection of a once-in-a-lifetime data center buildout & intensifying electrification of industry. Consensus would paint Exelon as a painfully slow, rate-case-dependent asset, but more than half of next year’s funding needs are already underwritten, and the comp is primed to deploy $38bn in capital through 2028 to drive the ~7.4% forward rate-base uplift.

Our $9.16bn FY25E base EBITDA (5.7% growth to $9.68bn FY26E) is deliberately substantially below Street - we exclude proximity Maryland noise and assume more disciplined regulatory cadence - but even at this base level, EXC’s mix of newly won RtD rates, density-driven load interconnection and O&M control drive fundamental earnings momentum with each 1% of rate-base growth accruing to EBITDA upside. Our 12.0x FY26E EV/EBITDA target multiple remains modestly below peer median given regulatory discretion but with asymmetric duration sensitivity as Treasury fade and utilities re-rate higher, while financing risk fades with management proactively underwriting ~60% required equity and padding interest expense future macro deterioration. The downside includes adverse regulation and re-accelerating rates, but these are largely priced-in and, in our view, already over-discounted. The asymmetric bear and bull scenarios are startling, with near-term execution de-risked and secular drivers just starting to be priced in. EXC offers one of the most compelling risk/reward set-ups for large cap regulated utilities today.

Data center load explosion

We view Exelon as the epicenter of a structural load transition, driven by accelerating mass data center proliferation across its footprint, and managing a record 17GW pipeline of large load interconnections, and an additional 16GW of high density, front-of-the-meter opportunities under advanced study — each load represents unprecedented capital deployment, regulatory cooperation, and customer engagement across the PJM territory. Our analysis shows these numbers signal not only aggregate utility load step-change, but a new base for multi-year rate-base growth, with 10% of the committed pipeline in-service by 2028 and nearly 75% in-service by 2034, implying a long tail of compounding earnings upside.

Critically, management is clear on none of this growth being tied to FERC-uncertain colocated load — with all projects aligned to incumbent transmission & distribution planning, avoiding ecosystem regulatory uncertainty altogether. The capital intensity is similarly compelling: of the $38bn four-year investment plan, $5bn is allocated to new business (itself a $900mn lift from the prior cycle), with an additional minimum of $1bn of incremental capex for large load explicitly signaled beyond 2028.
